sanskrit_devanagari:
तस्माद्यस्य महाबाहो निगृहीतानि सर्वशः ।
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ॥ ६८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
tasmād yasya mahā-bāho nigṛhītāni sarvaśaḥ |
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyas tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā || 68 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (canonical body text; standard sandhi indriyāṇi+indriyārthebhyaḥ → indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyaḥ)
exact (text body identical; punctuation differs — single danda)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.68।।)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.068 = MBh_06,024.068)

translations.verbatim_quote: Therefore, O mighty-armed, one whose senses are restrained from their objects is certainly of steady intelligence.
Therefore, one who has restrained the senses from their objects, O mighty armed Arjun, is firmly established in transcendental knowledge.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: One can curb the forces of sense gratification only by means of Kṛṣṇa consciousness, or engaging all the senses in the transcendental loving service of the Lord. As enemies are curbed by superior force, the senses can similarly be curbed, not by any human endeavor, but only by keeping them engaged in the service of the Lord.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The verse closes the immediate restatement triggered by 2.67. Where 2.67 named the loss-mechanism (mind drawn behind even one un-restrained sense), 2.68 names the positive: complete withdrawal of the senses from their objects yields established prajñā. Prabhupada specifically reads 'restraint' as engagement of the senses in devotional service rather than mere suppression — a Gaudiya Vaishnava reading that rejects pure negative renunciation.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Tasmat, therefore — for the reason given in the previous verse; mahabaho, O mighty-armed one; yasya, of him whose; indriyani, senses; are nigrhitani, restrained, withdrawn; sarvasah, from every side, completely; indriyarthebhyah, from their objects — sound and the rest; tasya, his, of that very man's; prajna, knowledge — discrimination of the Self; is pratisthita, established, steady.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 2.68 as the necessary positive corollary of 2.67's negative. The conjunction tasmāt is load-bearing: this verse is restating 2.61 in the form 2.67 has just demanded. The complete withdrawal (nigṛhītāni sarvaśaḥ) is what permits prajñā to be pratiṣṭhitā — etymologically 'standing-firmly-on'. The same word pratiṣṭhitā recurs through the sthita-prajña block (2.55, 2.57, 2.58, 2.61, 2.68) as the chapter's defining technical term.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: For the reasons given, the aspirant whose senses are entirely held back from their objects has prajñā that is pratiṣṭhitā — established. Without such withdrawal there can be no firmness in the discrimination of the Self that is the locus of devotion to the Lord; the verse therefore states a structural prerequisite, not an aesthetic ideal.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja agrees with Shankara on the structural reading: complete sense-withdrawal is the prerequisite, not the goal. Vishishtadvaita then adds the further specification that the established prajñā is itself oriented toward the Lord; pratiṣṭhitā is the firmness within which devotion can occur. The mighty-armed (mahā-bāho) vocative is read as honoring Arjuna's actual capacity for the discipline; it is not flattery but a statement that the addressee is capable of the restraint just prescribed.

positions.rendering: restrained from their objects (with the Gaudiya gloss: restraint = engagement in devotional service, not negative suppression)
restrained the senses from their objects
withdrawn from every side, completely (sarvaśaḥ as totality, not partial)
entirely held back, as the structural prerequisite for prajñā established in devotion

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four agree the verb is restraint/withdrawal and that sarvaśaḥ qualifies it as complete. They differ on the mechanism by which this restraint is achieved: Prabhupada (Gaudiya Vaishnava) explicitly rejects suppression-only and prescribes positive engagement of all senses in the Lord's service. Shankara reads it more strictly as withdrawal proper, on the path to ātma-anātma-viveka. Ramanuja sits between: withdrawal is the prerequisite, but the goal is firm devotion. Mukundananda flattens to the standard 'restrained the senses', tradition-neutral.

positions.rendering: knowledge — discrimination of the Self — established, steady

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: pratiṣṭhitā is the chapter's load-bearing technical term: the same root verb appears in sthita-prajña itself, in 2.55 ('sthita-prajñasya kā bhāṣā'), in 2.57 (sthitā tasya prajñā), in 2.58 (tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā), and now in 2.68. The repetition is structural — 2.68 is closing the immediate sub-argument by restating the chapter's defining state in the exact same lexical form. Translators preserve 'established' / 'firm' but generally do not flag the cross-verse repetition of the same compound; the source pack flags it for the doctrine-coherence check.

literal_meaning: Therefore, O mighty-armed, of him whose senses are restrained on every side from sense-objects, his discriminative wisdom is established.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna restates the conclusion that 2.61 first formulated and that 2.67 has just demanded in negative form: when the senses are withdrawn completely (sarvaśaḥ) from their objects, the discriminative wisdom (prajñā) is pratiṣṭhitā — firmly established. The conjunction tasmāt (therefore) makes the verse's logical position explicit: this is the positive corollary of the negative case-statement just given. Shankara reads prajñā here as ātma-anātma-viveka established as a stable state. Ramanuja preserves the same structural reading and adds that the established prajñā is the locus of devotion. Prabhupada (Gaudiya Vaishnava) further specifies that 'restraint' must be understood as positive engagement in the Lord's service, not mere suppression. The recurrence of pratiṣṭhitā ties this verse to 2.55, 2.57, 2.58, 2.61 — the chapter's defining technical term for the steady-mind state.

verbatim_quote_sources: bhagavad-gita.us (Ramanuja Gita-bhasya, Adidevananda tr. — paraphrase from JS-rendered page)
remaining_gaps: Ramanuja verbatim — page renders commentary in JS; only paraphrase recovered.
